topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed a monotonous tune above my head. I stared at the spreadsheet, my fingers hovering over the numbers, a knot tightening in my stomach. It was a routine quarterly review, nothing dramatic, just the usual performance metrics. Then I saw it—a line item detailing salary comparisons between departments. My gaze snagged on the figure next to Alex’s name, the newest analyst. The number was significantly higher than mine, a stark contrast to the hours I poured into my own projects. A strange, heavy feeling settled in my chest, like a stone had been dropped in. I quickly scrolled down, trying to rationalize it, to find some hidden metric that explained the discrepancy, but the data simply pointed to a reality I hadn't prepared for. I felt a sudden urge to look away, to hide the screen, the air suddenly feeling too thin to breathe.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" I managed to ask, my voice coming out a little too tight. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. Sarah had just finished presenting the departmental budget, and when she mentioned the compensation structure for the junior team, I felt a flush creep up my neck. I glanced over at Mark, who was quietly packing up his laptop. He looked so earnest, so driven, and the disparity between his pay and mine felt like a physical weight pressing down on my shoulders. I tried to focus on the next slide, on the projections, but my eyes kept drifting back to the screen, tracing the difference in figures. A dull ache started behind my eyes, and I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles turned white. I needed to process this, to figure out how to handle the sudden shift in perspective without letting it derail my entire afternoon.

[story 3]

It hit me like a cold splash of water. I reread the email confirmation, the stark figures laid out in black and white. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I felt a strange hollowness expand in my core. It wasn't anger that surfaced first, but a profound sense of imbalance. I looked toward the open doorway, half-expecting someone to walk in and challenge this reality. I swallowed hard, my throat suddenly dry. The reality was undeniable: someone else was earning substantially more for the same work. A wave of profound quiet washed over me, leaving me unsteady. I slowly closed the document, the sound of the click echoing loudly in the otherwise silent cubicle. I just wanted to disappear into the beige walls.
